meaning: adj.闈炲崠鍝佺殑,鏃犻攢璺殑
examples: The shares are unsaleable; we had better write them off.
The goods have been so discolored and heavily saturated by rain that they are unsaleable.
Moreover, average current assets had risen to 319 days-worth of annual sales, suggesting that most of the SOEs' working capital was tied up in uncollectable bills or unsaleable inventory.
The take away is that many popular schools of art fell out of favor and became unsaleable.
The result of this can be financially devastating: either because a painting proves to be a fake, or because a real painting, absent authentication, can be unsaleable.
